instructions: Extract hardcoded LLM model names out of the source code and into a centralized configuration. 1. Create a config module (e.g., `src/jitsu/config.py`) using `pydantic_settings.BaseSettings` to manage default model names for each agent type (e.g., `planner_model`, `executor_model`). 2. Configure the settings class to read from a root-level config file (like `.env` or `jitsu.toml`). Provide sensible defaults (e.g., 'openrouter/hunter-alpha'). 3. Update `src/jitsu/cli.py`, `src/jitsu/core/planner.py`, and `src/jitsu/core/executor.py` (or orchestrator) to read these models from the config instead of using hardcoded strings. 4. Ensure the CLI `--model` flag properly overrides the config at runtime. 5. Create `tests/test_config.py` and update existing tests to assert that models can be dynamically configured and overridden. Ensure 100% test coverage and no linting bypasses.
anti_patterns: Leaving any hardcoded provider/model strings in the core execution logic.
Failing to prioritize the CLI `--model` flag over the configured defaults.
Hardcoding the path to the config file in a way that breaks if jitsu is run from a different directory.
completion_criteria: Model names are loaded via a central Pydantic BaseSettings class in `config.py`.
The config natively supports reading from a root-level configuration file.
CLI and Core modules dynamically use these configured models based on agent type.
CLI flags correctly override config file values.
just verify passes cleanly with 100% test coverage.
